hero.title: Quantum materials under extreme conditions
hero.subtitle: We are an experimental condensed matter group studying quantum materials under extreme conditions, with a focus on unconventional superconductors, topological materials, and frustrated magnets.
hero.imageAlt: Quantum materials lab illustration with electron wave functions, atomic lattice, dilution refrigerator, and magnetic field lines

about.title: Experimental Physicists Exploring Quantum Materials
about.body: We discover, explore, and explain novel physics phenomena related to unconventional superconductors, topological materials, and frustrated magnets. We specialized in electrical transport, thermal transport, thermoelectric measurements, and torque differential magnetometry under extremely low temperatures and strong magnetic fields. Check out details in our

materials.title: Why Quantum Materials?
materials.intro: Modern technologies fundamentally rely on the properties of materials, yet many of these properties cannot be understood solely from classical physics but rather from collective quantum motions of excitations with strong correlations. The unique non-classical properties emerging from these interactions give rise to the so-called quantum materials, among which superconductors, quantum spin liquids, and topological materials represent three central classes. Together, they embody rich quantum physics while also offering promising opportunities for future quantum technologies and next-generation electronic devices.

overview.imageAlt: Three-dimensional bulk crystal lattice with metallic atoms, bonds, and translucent quantum electron-density waves.
overview.caption: Why Quantum materials ?
details: Modern technologies fundamentally rely on the properties of materials, yet many of these properties cannot be understood solely from classical physics or from the behavior of isolated atoms. In many solid-state and bulk material systems, physical properties emerge from the collective interplay among multiple degrees of freedom, including charge, spin, orbital, and lattice dynamics. These interactions give rise to strongly correlated excitations and emergent quantum phases that often require new theoretical frameworks and advanced experimental approaches for their understanding, verification, and prediction. Superconductors, quantum spin liquids, and topological materials represent three central classes of quantum materials. Together, they embody rich and unconventional condensed matter physics while also offering promising opportunities for future quantum technologies and next-generation electronic devices.

systems.title: Unconventional Superconductors
systems.summary: We focus on the nodal structure of the superconducting gap and the behavior of quasiparticles by measuring its low-temperature thermal conductivity and thermal Hall conductivity.
systems.imageAlt: YBCO phase diagram showing antiferromagnetic, pseudogap, and superconducting regions as temperature changes with carrier concentration.
systems.caption: Correlated phases can emerge from competing interactions in crystalline materials.
details: Increasing the superconducting transition temperature, \(T_c\), has long been regarded as one of the central directions for promoting broader technological applications of superconducting materials. Fortunately, \(T_c\) has been dramatically enhanced since the discovery of high-temperature superconductors. However, understanding the pairing mechanism in these unconventional superconductors—whose properties cannot be fully explained by the conventional Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer (BCS) theory—remains one of the holy grails in the field of quantum materials.

Beyond superconductivity itself, unconventional superconductors often exhibit a variety of other exotic phenomena, including quantum criticality, strong electronic correlations, magnetism, pseudogap phases, and charge- or spin-density-wave orders. The interplay among these competing and intertwined phases gives rise to remarkably rich and complicated phase diagrams. The particular question that we would like to focus on is the identification of the nodal structure of the superconducting gap using a powerful experimental technique: low-temperature thermal conductivity measurements. Determining the superconducting gap structure is crucial for identifying the symmetry of the superconducting order parameter and for revealing the microscopic pairing mechanism underlying unconventional superconductivity.

systems.title: Topological Materials
systems.summary: We study Fermi surface of topological materials using quantum oscillations and angle-dependent magnetoresistance (ADMR) under extreme high magnetic fields.
systems.imageAlt: Topological band structure schematic showing red and blue cones meeting at a point.
systems.caption: Band topology changes transport signatures in strong magnetic fields.
details: Topological materials are quantum materials whose electronic band structures possess nontrivial topological properties and protected electronic states. Classes of topological materials—including topological insulators, Dirac and Weyl semimetals, and topological superconductors—have emerged as promising platforms for next-generation electronic and quantum technologies. A central task in identifying and understanding topological materials is the determination of their Fermi surface structure and underlying electronic topology. The primary research problem that we aim to address is the detection and characterization of the Fermi surfaces of topological materials using high-magnetic-field experimental techniques, particularly quantum oscillation measurements and angle-dependent magnetoresistance (ADMR).

systems.title: Quantum Spin Liquids
systems.summary: Our lab searches for fractionalized excitations such as spinons and Majorana fermions by low-temperature thermal conductivity and thermal Hall measurements.
systems.imageAlt: Honeycomb lattice schematic with x, y, and z bonds and alternating spin arrows.
systems.caption: Frustrated spin systems can host fractionalized excitations without conventional order.
details: Quantum spin liquids (QSLs) are fascinating quantum materials that possess highly frustrated magnetic ground states and evade conventional magnetic ordering even at very low temperatures. The search for QSLs has attracted tremendous interest because of their potential to host non-Abelian statistics and other exotic fractionalized excitations, which could provide a foundation for fault-tolerant quantum computing. Although many materials have been proposed as candidate QSLs, none has yet been experimentally confirmed as a definitive realization of a true quantum spin liquid. Consequently, the discovery and experimental verification of achievable QSL materials remain among the major challenges in the field of quantum materials.

The identification and classification of QSL states in different candidate materials often rely on detecting and characterizing fractionalized excitations, such as spinons and Majorana fermions. The central questions that we aim to address are whether spinons can be detected through low-temperature thermal conductivity measurements and whether Majorana fermions can be identified using another powerful thermal transport technique, the thermal Hall effect.

tools.title: What are the Tools?
tools.intro: We employ a broad range of complementary low-temperature and high-magnetic-field experimental techniques to connect microscopic quantum states with macroscopic electrical, thermal, and magnetic properties in bulk quantum materials. Our primary experimental capabilities include electrical transport, thermal transport, torque differential magnetometry, and thermoelectric measurements, which together provide sensitive probes across multiple energy and length scales. In addition to advanced measurement capabilities, our group maintains an in-house crystal growth laboratory capable of synthesizing high-quality single crystals. The close integration of materials synthesis and precision measurements places our group on a unique and powerful platform for exploring fascinating physics in quantum materials.

items.title: Electrical Transport
items.summary: Angle-dependent magnetoresistance and related transport measurements connect carrier motion to Fermi-surface topology.
items.imageAlt: Angle-dependent electrical transport schematic showing current through a sample and magnetic field orientation defined by theta and phi angles.
items.caption: Figure_ADMR: Resistivity along c-axis changes when external B field points away from the crystal c-axis.
details: Angle-dependent magnetoresistance (ADMR) can be used to map a Fermi surface when quantum oscillations are not detected, e.g. because of low sample quality. ADMR refers to the change of the out-of-plane resistivity, i.e. c-axis resistivity, when an external magnetic field is applied and rotated away from the crystal c-axis towards the ab plane. As a semiclassical effect, the topology of the Fermi surface will translate into the trajectory of charge carriers, which in turn affects the electrical conductivity of materials in an external magnetic field.

items.title: Thermoelectric Measurements
items.summary: Seebeck and Nernst measurements reveal longitudinal and transverse voltage responses to thermal gradients and magnetic fields.
items.imageAlt: Schematic showing longitudinal and transverse thermal voltage measurements under a thermal gradient and magnetic field.
items.caption: Figure: Thermoelectric probes separate longitudinal and Hall-like thermal responses.
details: The thermoelectric measurements include Seebeck and Nernst effects. Seebeck effect describes a longitudinal voltage generated by a longitudinal thermal gradient \(V_{th}\). Nernst effect is the appearance of a transverse voltage \(V_{Hall}\) when a longitudinal thermal gradient and perpendicular magnetic field are applied.

items.title: Thermal Transport
items.summary: Low-temperature thermal conductivity and thermal Hall conductivity probe charged and neutral excitations that carry entropy in quantum materials.
items.imageAlt: Thermal transport schematic showing a sample between a heater and heat sink with magnetic field and temperature gradients.
items.caption: Figure: Thermal Hall Effect: \(\Delta T_y\) induced by \(\Delta T_x\) in the presence of \(H\)
details: We use both low-temperature thermal conductivity and thermal Hall conductivity measurements to probe charged and neutral excitations that carry entropy in various quantum materials. The thermal Hall effect is the thermal analog of the electrical Hall effect. Instead of a transverse voltage induced by a perpendicular magnetic field in the presence of an electric current, a transverse temperature difference is induced in the presence of a heat current.

items.title: Torque Differential Magnetometry
items.summary: A qPlus-mode quartz tuning fork platform measures small magnetic signals at millikelvin temperatures and high magnetic fields.
items.imageAlt: Quartz tuning fork schematic with a sample mounted on a substrate and gold wires.
items.caption: Figure: Schematic of tuning fork based torque differential magnetometry.
details: A highly sensitive torque differential magnetometry using the qPlus mode of a miniature-sized quartz tuning fork has been developed to measure small magnetic signals down to a dilution refrigerator temperature of 25 mK and a strong magnetic field up to 65 T. We have successfully applied this technique under vacuum, in a liquid helium environment, in a DC or pulsed magnetic field.

items.title: Single Crystal Growth
items.summary: High-quality crystals are grown using solid-state reaction, chemical vapor transport, flux growth, and related methods.
items.imageAlt: Microscope view of dark faceted single crystals on a red grid background.
items.caption: Figure: High-quality single crystals grown by chemical vapor transport.
details: High-quality single crystals provide definitive platform to resolve conflicting results in bulk materials, enabling intrinsic physical properties to be measured without extrinsic interference. Our lab specializes in their growth through a range of techniques, including solid-state reaction, chemical vapor transport (CVT), flux growth, and other related methods.

lab.title: Facilities and Platforms
lab.intro: The lab is being built across dedicated spaces for low-temperature and high-field measurements, sample preparation, and shared single crystal growth facilities.

panels.title: MRL B09: Measurement lab
paragraphs: The basement measurement lab will host a Quantum Design Oxford ProteoxMX dilution refrigerator and a Quantum Design Oxford TeslatronPT system, each with a 14 T magnet.
Platforms support electrical transport, thermal transport, thermoelectric, torque differential magnetometry at low temperature and high field.

photos.alt: MRL B09 measurement lab during renovation
Future MRL B09 measurement lab photo placeholder

panels.title: ESB 120: Sample growth facility
paragraphs: This shared centralized growth facility will host multiple muffle, tube, two-zone, and optical floating-zone furnaces plus a glove box.
Methods include solid-state reaction, chemical vapor transport, flux growth, and floating-zone synthesis.

photos.alt: ESB 120 shared sample growth facility during renovation
Future ESB 120 sample growth facility photo placeholder

panels.title: MRL 234: Sample preparation lab
paragraphs: This lab supports sample preparation under microscopes.
Sample preparation processes include cutting, polishing, mounting, and contacting samples for various types of experiments.

photos.alt: MRL 234 sample preparation lab with microscope workstations
Future MRL 234 sample preparation lab photo placeholder

publications.title: Thermal Hall conductivity of electron-doped cuprates: Electrons and phonons
publications.authors: Marie-Eve Boulanger, Lu Chen, Vincent Oliviero, David Vignolles, Gaël Grissonnanche, Ke-Jun Xu, Zhi-Xun Shen, Cyril Proust, Jordan Baglo, Louis Taillefer
publications.abstract: It has recently become clear that phonons generate a sizable thermal Hall effect in cuprates, whether they are undoped, electron-doped or hole-doped (inside the pseudogap phase). At higher doping, where cuprates are reasonably good metals, mobile electrons also generate a thermal Hall effect, the thermal equivalent of the standard electrical Hall effect. Here we show that in the cleanest crystals of the electron-doped cuprate \(\mathrm{Nd}_{2-x}\mathrm{Ce}_{x}\mathrm{CuO}_{4}\), at high doping, the phonon and electron contributions to the thermal Hall conductivity \(\kappa_{xy}\) are of comparable magnitude, but of opposite sign. In samples of lower quality, phonons dominate \(\kappa_{xy}\), resulting in a negative \(\kappa_{xy}\) at all temperatures. The fact that the negative phononic \(\kappa_{xy}\) in the metallic state is similar in magnitude and temperature dependence to that found in the insulating state at lower doping rules out any mechanism based on skew scattering of phonons off charged impurities, since a local charge should be screened in the metallic regime. The phononic \(\kappa_{xy}\) is found to persist over the entire doping range where antiferromagnetic correlations are known to be significant, suggesting that such correlations may play a role in generating the phonon thermal Hall effect in electron-doped cuprates. If the same mechanism is also at play in hole-doped cuprates, the presence of a phononic \(\kappa_{xy}\) below (and only below) the critical doping \(p^{\star}\) would be evidence that spin correlations are a property of the pseudogap phase.

publications.title: Planar Thermal Hall Effect from Phonons in Cuprates
publications.authors: Lu Chen, Léna Le Roux, Gaël Grissonnanche, Marie-Eve Boulanger, Steven Thériault, Ruixing Liang, D. A. Bonn, W. N. Hardy, S. Pyon, T. Takayama, H. Takagi, Ke-Jun Xu, Zhi-Xun Shen, Louis Taillefer
publications.abstract: A surprising “planar” thermal Hall effect, whereby the field is parallel to the current, has recently been observed in a few magnetic insulators; this effect has been attributed to exotic excitations such as Majorana fermions or chiral magnons. Here, we investigate the possibility of a planar thermal Hall effect in three different cuprate materials, in which the conventional thermal Hall conductivity \(\kappa_{xy}\) (with an out-of-plane field perpendicular to the current) is dominated by either electrons or phonons. Our measurements show that the planar \(\kappa_{xy}\) from electrons in cuprates is zero, as expected from the absence of a Lorentz force in the planar configuration. By contrast, we observe a sizable planar \(\kappa_{xy}\) in those samples where the thermal Hall response is due to phonons, even though it should, in principle, be forbidden by the high crystal symmetry. Our findings call for a careful reexamination of the mechanisms responsible for the phonon thermal Hall effect in insulators.

publications.title: Role of magnetic ions in the thermal Hall effect of the paramagnetic insulator \(\mathbf{TmVO_4}\) test
publications.authors: Ashvini Vallipuram, Lu Chen, Emma Campillo, Manel Mezidi, Gaël Grissonnanche, Marie-Eve Boulanger, Étienne Lefrançois, Mark P. Zic, Yuntian Li, Ian R. Fisher, Jordan Baglo, Louis Taillefer
publications.abstract: In a growing number of materials, phonons have been found to generate a thermal Hall effect, but the underlying mechanism remains unclear. Inspired by previous studies that revealed the importance of \(\mathrm{Tb}^{3+}\) ions in generating the thermal Hall effect in a family of pyrochlores, we investigated the role of \(\mathrm{Tm}^{3+}\) ions in \(\mathrm{TmVO}_{4}\), a paramagnetic insulator with a different crystal structure. We observe a negative thermal Hall conductivity in \(\mathrm{TmVO}_{4}\) with a magnitude such that the Hall angle, \(|\kappa_{xy}/\kappa_{xx}|\), is approximately \(1 \times 10^{-3}\) at \(H = 15\,\mathrm{T}\) and \(T = 20\,\mathrm{K}\), typical for a phonon-generated thermal Hall effect. In contrast to the negligible \(\kappa_{xy}\) found in the nonmagnetic pyrochlore analog (where the \(\mathrm{Tb}^{3+}\) ions are replaced with \(\mathrm{Y}^{3+}\)), we observe a negative \(\kappa_{xy}\) in \(\mathrm{YVO}_{4}\) with a Hall angle of magnitude comparable to that of \(\mathrm{TmVO}_{4}\). This shows that the \(\mathrm{Tm}^{3+}\) ions are not essential for the thermal Hall effect in this family of materials. Interestingly, at an intermediate Y concentration of \(x = 0.3\) in \(\mathrm{Tm}_{1-x}\mathrm{Y}_{x}\mathrm{VO}_{4}\), \(\kappa_{xy}\) was found to have a positive sign, pointing to the importance of impurities in the thermal Hall effect of phonons.

publications.title: Planar thermal Hall effect from phonons in a Kitaev candidate material
publications.authors: Lu Chen, Étienne Lefrançois, Ashvini Vallipuram, Quentin Barthélemy, Amirreza Ataei, Weiliang Yao, Yuan Li, Louis Taillefer
publications.abstract: The thermal Hall effect has emerged as a potential probe of exotic excitations in spin liquids. In the Kitaev magnet \(\alpha\text{-}\mathrm{RuCl}_{3}\), the thermal Hall conductivity \(\kappa_{xy}\) has been attributed to Majorana fermions, chiral magnons, or phonons. Theoretically, the former two types of heat carriers can generate a “planar” \(\kappa_{xy}\), whereby the magnetic field is parallel to the heat current, but it is unknown whether phonons also could. Here we show that a planar \(\kappa_{xy}\) is present in another Kitaev candidate material, \(\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{Co}_{2}\mathrm{TeO}_{6}\). Based on the striking similarity between \(\kappa_{xy}\) and the phonon-dominated thermal conductivity \(\kappa_{xx}\), we attribute the effect to phonons. We observe a large difference in \(\kappa_{xy}\) between different configurations of heat current and magnetic field, which reveals that the direction of heat current matters in determining the planar \(\kappa_{xy}\). Our observation calls for a re-evaluation of the planar \(\kappa_{xy}\) observed in \(\alpha\text{-}\mathrm{RuCl}_{3}\).

publications.title: Phonon chirality from impurity scattering in the antiferromagnetic phase of \(\mathbf{Sr_2IrO_4}\)
publications.authors: A. Ataei, G. Grissonnanche, M.-E. Boulanger, L. Chen, É. Lefrançois, V. Brouet, L. Taillefer
publications.abstract: A thermal Hall effect occurs in an increasing number of insulators and is often attributed to phonons, but the underlying mechanism is not known in most cases. Two main scenarios have been proposed: either a coupling of phonons to spins or scattering of phonons by impurities or defects, but there is no systematic evidence to support either of them. Here we present evidence for the phonon impurity scattering picture by studying the effect of adding rhodium impurities to the antiferromagnetic insulator \(\mathrm{Sr}_{2}\mathrm{IrO}_{4}\), substituting for the spin-carrying iridium atoms. We find that adding small concentrations of rhodium impurities increases the thermal Hall conductivity, but adding enough rhodium to suppress the magnetic order eventually decreases it until it nearly vanishes. In contrast, introducing lanthanum impurities that substitute for the strontium atoms, which lie outside the \(\mathrm{IrO}_{2}\) planes that are the seat of magnetism, produces a much smaller enhancement of the thermal Hall conductivity. We conclude that the thermal Hall effect in this material is caused by the scattering of phonons by impurities embedded within a magnetic environment.

publications.title: Reconciling scaling of the optical conductivity of cuprate superconductors with Planckian resistivity and specific heat
publications.authors: Bastien Michon, Christophe Berthod, Carl Willem Rischau, Amirreza Ataei, Lu Chen, Seiki Komiya, Shimpei Ono, Louis Taillefer, Dirk van der Marel, Antoine Georges
publications.abstract: Materials tuned to a quantum critical point display universal scaling properties as a function of temperature T and frequency \(\omega\). A long-standing puzzle regarding cuprate superconductors has been the observed power-law dependence of optical conductivity with an exponent smaller than one, in contrast to T-linear dependence of the resistivity and \(\omega\)-linear dependence of the optical scattering rate. Here, we present and analyze resistivity and optical conductivity of \(\mathrm{La}_{2-x}\mathrm{Sr}_{x}\mathrm{CuO}_{4}\) with x = 0.24. We demonstrate \(\hbar\omega/k_B T\) scaling of the optical data over a wide range of frequency and temperature, T-linear resistivity, and optical effective mass proportional to \(\sim \ln T\) corroborating previous specific heat experiments. We show that a T, \(\omega\)-linear scaling Ansatz for the inelastic scattering rate leads to a unified theoretical description of the experimental data, including the power-law of the optical conductivity. This theoretical framework provides new opportunities for describing the unique properties of quantum critical matter.

publications.title: Large phonon thermal Hall conductivity in the antiferromagnetic insulator \(\mathbf{Cu_3TeO_6}\)
publications.authors: Lu Chen, Marie-Eve Boulanger, Zhi-Cheng Wang, Fazel Tafti, Louis Taillefer
publications.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 119(34), e2208016119 (2022)

links.label: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

publications.abstract: Phonons are known to generate a thermal Hall effect in certain insulators, including oxides with rare-earth impurities, quantum paraelectrics, multiferroic materials, and cuprate Mott insulators. In each case, a special feature of the material is presumed relevant for the underlying mechanism that confers chirality to phonons in a magnetic field. A fundamental question is whether a phonon Hall effect is an unusual occurrence—linked to special characteristics such as skew scattering off rare-earth impurities, structural domains, ferroelectricity, or ferromagnetism—or a much more common property of insulators than hitherto believed. To help answer this question, we have turned to a material with none of the previously encountered special features: the cubic antiferromagnet \(\mathrm{Cu}_{3}\mathrm{TeO}_{6}\). We find that its thermal Hall conductivity \(\kappa_{xy}\) is among the largest of any insulator so far. We show that this record-high \(\kappa_{xy}\) signal is due to phonons, and it does not require the presence of magnetic order, as it persists above the ordering temperature. We conclude that the phonon Hall effect is likely to be a fairly common property of solids.

publications.title: Magnetic field effects on the quantum spin liquid behaviors of NaYbS₂
publications.authors: Jiangtao Wu, Jianshu Li, Zheng Zhang, Changle Liu, Yong Hao Gao, Erxi Feng, Guochu Deng, Qingyong Ren, Zhe Wang, Rui Chen, Jan Embs, Fengfeng Zhu, Qing Huang, Ziji Xiang, Lu Chen, Yan Wu, E. S. Choi, Zhe Qu, Lu Li, Junfeng Wang, Haidong Zhou, Yixi Su, Xiaoqun Wang, Gang Chen, Qingming Zhang, Jie Ma
publications.abstract: Spin-orbit coupling is an important ingredient to regulate the many-body physics, especially for many spin liquid candidate materials such as rare-earth magnets and Kitaev materials. The rare-earth chalcogenides \(\mathrm{NaYbCh}_{2}\) (Ch = O, S, Se) is a congenital frustrating system to exhibit the intrinsic landmark of spin liquid by eliminating both the site disorders between \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\) and \(\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}\) ions with the big ionic size difference and the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction with the perfect triangular lattice of the \(\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}\) ions. The temperature versus magnetic-field phase diagram is established by the magnetization, specific heat, and neutron-scattering measurements. Notably, the neutron diffraction spectra and the magnetization curve might provide microscopic evidence for a series of spin configuration for in-plane fields, which include the disordered spin liquid state, 120° antiferromagnet, and one-half magnetization state. Furthermore, the ground state is suggested to be a gapless spin liquid from inelastic neutron scattering, and the magnetic field adjusts the spin orbit coupling. Therefore, the strong spin-orbit coupling in the frustrated quantum magnet substantially enriches low-energy spin physics. This rare-earth family could offer a good platform for exploring the quantum spin liquid ground state and quantum magnetic transitions.

publications.title: Electrons with Planckian scattering obey standard orbital motion in a magnetic field
publications.authors: Amirreza Ataei, A. Gourgout, G. Grissonnanche, L. Chen, J. Baglo, M.-E. Boulanger, F. Laliberté, S. Badoux, N. Doiron-Leyraud, V. Oliviero, S. Benhabib, D. Vignolles, J.-S. Zhou, S. Ono, H. Takagi, C. Proust, Louis Taillefer
publications.abstract: In various so-called strange metals, electrons undergo Planckian dissipation [1], [2], a strong and anomalous scattering that grows linearly with temperature [3], in contrast to the quadratic temperature dependence expected from the standard theory of metals. In some cuprates [4], [5] and pnictides [6], a linear dependence of resistivity on a magnetic field has also been considered anomalous—possibly an additional facet of Planckian dissipation. Here we show that the resistivity of the cuprate strange metals \(\mathrm{Nd}_{0.4}\mathrm{La}_{1.6-x}\mathrm{Sr}_{x}\mathrm{CuO}_{4}\) (ref. 7) and \(\mathrm{La}_{2-x}\mathrm{Sr}_{x}\mathrm{CuO}_{4}\) (ref. 8) is quantitatively consistent with the standard Boltzmann theory of electron motion in a magnetic field, in all aspects—field strength, field direction, temperature and disorder level. The linear field dependence is found to be simply the consequence of scattering rate anisotropy. We conclude that Planckian dissipation is anomalous in its temperature dependence, but not in its field dependence. The scattering rate in these cuprates does not depend on field, which means that their Planckian dissipation is robust against fields up to at least 85 T.

abstractCitations.label: Similarity of scattering rates in metals showing T-linear resistivity · Science

Linear-in-temperature resistivity from an isotropic Planckian scattering rate · Nature
Scale-invariant magnetoresistance in a cuprate superconductor · Science
Incoherent transport across the strange-metal regime of overdoped cuprates · Nature
Scaling between magnetic field and temperature in the high-temperature superconductor \(\mathrm{BaFe}_{2}(\mathrm{As}_{1-x}\mathrm{P}_{x})_{2}\) · Nature Physics

Anomalous criticality in the electrical resistivity of \(\mathrm{La}_{2-x}\mathrm{Sr}_{x}\mathrm{CuO}_{4}\) · Science

publications.title: Hall Anomaly, Quantum Oscillations and Possible Lifshitz Transitions in Kondo Insulator \(\mathbf{YbB_{12}}\): Evidence for Unconventional Charge Transport
publications.authors: Ziji Xiang, Kuan-Wen Chen, Lu Chen, Tomoya Asaba, Yuki Sato, Nan Zhang, Dechen Zhang, Yuichi Kasahara, Fumitoshi Iga, William A. Coniglio, Yuji Matsuda, John Singleton, Lu Li
publications.abstract: In correlated electronic systems, strong interactions and the interplay between different degrees of freedom may give rise to anomalous charge transport properties, which can be tuned by external parameters like temperature and magnetic field. Recently, magnetic quantum oscillations and metallic low-temperature thermal conductivity have been observed in the Kondo insulator \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\), whose resistivity is a few orders of magnitude higher than those of conventional metals. As yet, these unusual observations are not fully understood. Here we present a detailed investigation of the behavior of \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\) under intense magnetic fields using both transport and torque magnetometry measurements. A low-field Hall anomaly, reminiscent of the Hall response associated with "strange-metal" physics, develops at \(T < 1.5\,\mathrm{K}\). At two characteristic magnetic fields (\(\mu_0 H_1 = 19.6\,\mathrm{T}\) and \(\mu_0 H_2 \sim 31\,\mathrm{T}\)), signatures appear in the Hall coefficient, magnetic torque, and magnetoresistance. We suggest that they are likely to be field-induced Lifshitz transitions. Moreover, above 35 T, the background resistivity displays an unusual, nonmetallic \(T^{\alpha}\)-behavior, with \(\alpha\) being field-dependent and varying between -1.5 and -2. By normalizing the Shubnikov-de Haas oscillation amplitude to this \(T^{\alpha}\)-dependence, the calculated cyclotron mass becomes more consistent with that deduced from de Haas-van Alphen oscillations. Our results support a novel two-fluid scenario in \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\): a Fermi-liquid-like fluid of charge-neutral quasiparticles coexists with charge carriers that remain in a nonmetallic state. The former experience successive Lifshitz transitions and develop Landau quantization in applied magnetic fields, whilst scattering between both fluids allows the Shubnikov-de Haas effect to be observed in the electrical transport.

publications.title: Seebeck Coefficient in a Cuprate Superconductor: Particle-Hole Asymmetry in the Strange Metal Phase and Fermi Surface Transformation in the Pseudogap Phase
publications.authors: A. Gourgout, G. Grissonnanche, F. Laliberté, A. Ataei, L. Chen, S. Verret, J.-S. Zhou, J. Mravlje, A. Georges, N. Doiron-Leyraud, Louis Taillefer
publications.abstract: We report measurements of the Seebeck effect in both the \(ab\) plane (\(S_a\)) and along the \(c\) axis (\(S_c\)) of the cuprate superconductor \(\mathrm{La}_{1.6-x}\mathrm{Nd}_{0.4}\mathrm{Sr}_{x}\mathrm{CuO}_{4}\) (Nd-LSCO), performed in magnetic fields large enough to suppress superconductivity down to low temperature. We use the Seebeck coefficient as a probe of the particle-hole asymmetry of the electronic structure across the pseudogap critical doping \(p^{\star} = 0.23\). Outside the pseudogap phase, at \(p = 0.24 > p^{\star}\), we observe a positive and essentially isotropic Seebeck coefficient as \(T \to 0\). That \(S > 0\) at \(p = 0.24\) is at odds with expectations given the electronic band structure of Nd-LSCO above \(p^{\star}\) and its known electronlike Fermi surface. We can reconcile this observation by invoking an energy-dependent scattering rate with a particle-hole asymmetry, possibly rooted in the non-Fermi-liquid nature of cuprates just above \(p^{\star}\). Inside the pseudogap phase, for \(p < p^{\star}\), \(S_a\) is seen to rise at low temperature as previously reported, consistent with the drop in carrier density 𝑛 from \(n \simeq 1 + p\) to \(n \simeq p\) across \(p^{\star}\) as inferred from other transport properties. In stark contrast, \(S_c\) at low temperature becomes negative below \(p^{\star}\), a novel signature of the pseudogap phase. The sudden drop in \(S_c\) reveals a change in the electronic structure of Nd-LSCO upon crossing \(p^{\star}\). We can exclude a profound change of the scattering across \(p^{\star}\) and conclude that the change in the out-of-plane Seebeck coefficient originates from a transformation of the Fermi surface.

publications.title: Survival of itinerant excitations and quantum spin state transitions in \(\mathbf{YbMgGaO_4}\) with chemical disorder
publications.authors: X. Rao, G. Hussain, Q. Huang, W. J. Chu, N. Li, X. Zhao, Z. Dun, E. S. Choi, T. Asaba, L. Chen, L. Li, X. Y. Yue, N. N. Wang, J.-G. Cheng, Y. H. Gao, Y. Shen, J. Zhao, G. Chen, H. D. Zhou, X. F. Sun
publications.abstract: A recent focus of quantum spin liquid (QSL) studies is how disorder/randomness in a QSL candidate affects its true magnetic ground state. The ultimate question is whether the QSL survives disorder or the disorder leads to a “spin-liquid-like” state, such as the proposed random-singlet (RS) state. Since disorder is a standard feature of most QSL candidates, this question represents a major challenge for QSL candidates. \(\mathrm{YbMgGaO}_{4}\), a triangular lattice antiferromagnet with effective spin-\(1/2\) \(\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}\) ions, is an ideal system to address this question, since it shows no long-range magnetic ordering with Mg/Ga site disorder. Despite the intensive study, it remains unresolved as to whether \(\mathrm{YbMgGaO}_{4}\) is a QSL or in the RS state. Here, through ultralow-temperature thermal conductivity and magnetic torque measurements, plus specific heat and DC magnetization data, we observed a residual \(\kappa_0/T\) term and series of quantum spin state transitions in the zero temperature limit for \(\mathrm{YbMgGaO}_{4}\). These observations strongly suggest that a QSL state with itinerant excitations and quantum spin fluctuations survives disorder in \(\mathrm{YbMgGaO}_{4}\).

publications.title: Topological surface conduction in Kondo insulator \(\mathbf{YbB_{12}}\)
publications.authors: Y. Sato, Z. Xiang, Y. Kasahara, S. Kasahara, L. Chen, C. Tinsman, F. Iga, J. Singleton, N. L. Nair, N. Maksimovic, J. G. Analytis, Lu Li, Y. Matsuda
publications.venue: Journal of Physics D: Applied Physics 54(40), 404002 (2021)

links.label: Journal of Physics D: Applied Physics

publications.abstract: Kondo insulators have recently aroused great interest because they are promising materials that host a topological insulator state caused by the strong electron interactions. Moreover, recent observations of the quantum oscillations in the insulating state of Kondo insulators have come as a great surprise. Here, to investigate the surface electronic state of a prototype Kondo insulator \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\), we measured the transport properties of single crystals and microstructures. In all samples, the temperature dependence of the electrical resistivity is insulating at high temperatures and the resistivity exhibits a plateau at low temperatures. The magnitude of the plateau value decreases with reducing sample thickness, which is quantitatively consistent with the surface electronic conduction in the bulk insulating \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\). Moreover, the magnetoresistance of the microstructures exhibits a weak-antilocalization effect at low field. These results are consistent with the presence of a topologically protected surface state, suggesting that \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\) is a candidate material for a topological Kondo insulator. The high field resistivity measurements up to \(\mu_0 H = 50\,\mathrm{T}\) of the microstructures provide supporting evidence that the quantum oscillations of the resistivity in \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\) occurs in the insulating bulk.

publications.title: Unusual high-field metal in a Kondo insulator
publications.authors: Ziji Xiang, Lu Chen, Kuan-Wen Chen, Colin Tinsman, Yuki Sato, Tomoya Asaba, Helen Lu, Yuichi Kasahara, Marcelo Jaime, Fedor Balakirev, Fumitoshi Iga, Yuji Matsuda, John Singleton, Lu Li
publications.abstract: Strong electronic interactions in condensed-matter systems often lead to unusual quantum phases. One such phase occurs in the Kondo insulator \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\), the insulating state of which exhibits phenomena that are characteristic of metals, such as magnetic quantum oscillations [1], a gapless fermionic contribution to heat capacity [2], [3] and itinerant-fermion thermal transport [3]. To understand these phenomena, it is informative to study their evolution as the energy gap of the Kondo insulator state is closed by a large magnetic field. Here we show that clear quantum oscillations are observed in the resulting high-field metallic state in \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\); this is despite it possessing relatively high resistivity, large effective masses and huge Kadowaki–Woods ratio, a combination that normally precludes quantum oscillations. Both quantum oscillation frequency and cyclotron mass display a strong field dependence. By tracking the Fermi surface area, we conclude that the same quasiparticle band gives rise to quantum oscillations in both insulating and metallic states. These data are understood most simply by using a two-fluid picture in which neutral quasiparticles—contributing little or nothing to charge transport—coexist with charged fermions. Our observations of the complex field-dependent behaviour of the fermion ensemble inhabiting \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\) provide strong constraints for existing theoretical models.

abstractCitations.label: Quantum oscillations of electrical resistivity in an insulator · Science
Magnetic-field-induced Kondo metal realized in \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\) · Physical Review Letters
Unconventional thermal metallic state of charge-neutral fermions in an insulator · Nature Physics

publications.title: Spontaneous Nernst effect in the iron-based superconductor \(\mathbf{Fe_{1+y}Te_{1-x}Se_x}\)
publications.authors: Lu Chen, Ziji Xiang, Colin Tinsman, Bin Lei, Xianhui Chen, G. D. Gu, Lu Li
publications.abstract: We present a study of the Nernst effect in an iron-based superconductor with a nontrivial band topology \({\mathrm{Fe}}_{1+y}{\mathrm{Te}}_{1-x}{\mathrm{Se}}_{x}\). A nonzero Nernst signal is observed in a narrow temperature region around the superconducting transition temperature \({T}_{c}\) at a zero field. This anomalous Nernst signal shows symmetric dependence on the external magnetic field and indicates an unconventional vortex contribution in an \(s\)-wave superconductor with a strong spin-orbit coupling, which is originated from the local magnetic moments of the interstitial Fe atoms. Our experiments also provide the first evidence of a locally broken time-reversal symmetry in bulk \({\mathrm{Fe}}_{1+y}{\mathrm{Te}}_{1-x}{\mathrm{Se}}_{x}\) single crystals.

publications.title: Unconventional thermal metallic state of charge-neutral fermions in an insulator
publications.authors: Y. Sato, Z. Xiang, Y. Kasahara, T. Taniguchi, S. Kasahara, L. Chen, T. Asaba, C. Tinsman, H. Murayama, O. Tanaka, Y. Mizukami, T. Shibauchi, F. Iga, J. Singleton, Lu Li, Y. Matsuda
publications.abstract: Quantum oscillations in transport and thermodynamic parameters at high magnetic fields are an unambiguous signature of the Fermi surface, the defining characteristic of a metal. Recent observations of quantum oscillations in insulating \(\mathrm{SmB}_{6}\) and \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\), therefore, have been a big surprise—despite the large charge gap inferred from the insulating behaviour of the resistivity, these compounds seemingly host a Fermi surface at high magnetic fields. However, the nature of the ground state in zero field has been little explored. Here, we report the use of low-temperature heat-transport measurements to discover gapless, itinerant, charge-neutral excitations in the ground state of \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\). At zero field, sizeable linear temperature-dependent terms in the heat capacity and thermal conductivity are clearly resolved in the zero-temperature limit, indicating the presence of gapless fermionic excitations with an itinerant character. Remarkably, linear temperature-dependent thermal conductivity leads to a spectacular violation of the Wiedemann–Franz law: the Lorenz ratio is \(10^{4}\)–\(10^{5}\) times larger than that expected in conventional metals, indicating that \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\) is a charge insulator and a thermal metal. Moreover, we find that these fermions couple to magnetic fields, despite their charge neutrality. Our findings expose novel quasiparticles in this unconventional quantum state.

publications.title: Anomalous thermal conductivity across the structural transition in \(\mathbf{SmBaMn_2O_6}\) single crystals
publications.authors: Lu Chen, Ziji Xiang, Colin Tinsman, Qing Huang, K. G. Reynolds, Haidong Zhou, Lu Li
publications.abstract: To understand whether the enhancement of thermal conductivity could take place at different types of phase transitions, we conduct thermal measurements in \(\mathrm{SmBaMn}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{6}\) single crystals which have both structural and magnetic phase transitions. In \(\mathrm{SmBaMn}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{6}\), successive phase transitions in charge, spin, and lattice degrees of freedoms take place at \(T_{\mathrm{CO1}} \sim 362\,\mathrm{K}\), \(T_{\mathrm{CO2}} \sim 190\,\mathrm{K}\), and \(T_N \sim 175\,\mathrm{K}\). An enhancement of thermal conductivity is only observed at the structural transition around \(T_{\mathrm{CO1}}\) while it is absent at the structural transition around \(T_{\mathrm{CO2}}\). One possible explanation is that the spin-phonon coupling in this material strongly modifies the phonon spectra and prevents the evolution of soft-phonon modes around the antiferromagnetic transition \(T_N\), which lies right below \(T_{\mathrm{CO2}}\). Our study provides a way to look for enhanced thermal conductivity in materials where multiple phase transitions coexist.

publications.title: Magnetic Field Enhanced Superconductivity in Epitaxial Thin Film \(\mathbf{WTe_2}\)
publications.authors: Tomoya Asaba, Yongjie Wang, Gang Li, Ziji Xiang, Colin Tinsman, Lu Chen, Shangnan Zhou, Songrui Zhao, David Laleyan, Yi Li, Zetian Mi, Lu Li
publications.abstract: In conventional superconductors an external magnetic field generally suppresses superconductivity. This results from a simple thermodynamic competition of the superconducting and magnetic free energies. In this study, we report the unconventional features in the superconducting epitaxial thin film tungsten telluride (\(\mathrm{WTe}_{2}\)). Measuring the electrical transport properties of Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) grown \(\mathrm{WTe}_{2}\) thin films with a high precision rotation stage, we map the upper critical field \(H_{c2}\) at different temperatures \(T\). We observe the superconducting transition temperature \(T_c\) is enhanced by in-plane magnetic fields. The upper critical field \(H_{c2}\) is observed to establish an unconventional non-monotonic dependence on temperature. We suggest that this unconventional feature is due to the lifting of inversion symmetry, which leads to the enhancement of \(H_{c2}\) in Ising superconductors.

publications.title: Quantum oscillations of electrical resistivity in an insulator
publications.authors: Z. Xiang, Y. Kasahara, T. Asaba, B. Lawson, C. Tinsman, Lu Chen, K. Sugimoto, S. Kawaguchi, Y. Sato, G. Li, S. Yao, Y. L. Chen, F. Iga, John Singleton, Y. Matsuda, Lu Li
publications.abstract: In metals, orbital motions of conduction electrons on the Fermi surface are quantized in magnetic fields, which is manifested by quantum oscillations in electrical resistivity. This Landau quantization is generally absent in insulators. Here, we report a notable exception in an insulator—ytterbium dodecaboride (\(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\)). The resistivity of \(\mathrm{YbB}_{12}\), which is of a much larger magnitude than the resistivity in metals, exhibits distinct quantum oscillations. These unconventional oscillations arise from the insulating bulk, even though the temperature dependence of the oscillation amplitude follows the conventional Fermi liquid theory of metals with a large effective mass. Quantum oscillations in the magnetic torque are also observed, albeit with a lighter effective mass.

publications.title: Enhancement of thermal conductivity across the metal-insulator transition in vanadium dioxide
publications.authors: Lu Chen, Ziji Xiang, Colin Tinsman, Tomoya Asaba, Qing Huang, Haidong Zhou, Lu Li
publications.abstract: Metal-to-insulator transition (MIT) in vanadium dioxide (\(\mathrm{VO}_{2}\)) was investigated by electrical and thermal transport measurements. We report an order-of-magnitude enhancement of thermal conductivity across the MIT region in the \(\mathrm{VO}_{2}\) single crystal. The magnetic field dependent measurement reveals that the thermal conductivity peak does not show an obvious dependence on the magnetic field, which indicates that the enhancement of thermal conductivity could come from neutral heat carriers such as phonons. Our experiment provides a direction of achieving thermal management in phase-change materials.

publications.title: Torque Differential Magnetometry Using the qPlus Mode of a Quartz Tuning Fork
publications.authors: Lu Chen, Fan Yu, Ziji Xiang, Tomoya Asaba, Colin Tinsman, Benjamin Lawson, Paul M. Sass, Weida Wu, B. L. Kang, Xianhui Chen, Lu Li
publications.abstract: A quartz tuning fork is the key component of high-resolution atomic force microscope. Because of its high quality factor, a quartz tuning fork can also be used for high-sensitivity magnetometry. We develop a highly sensitive torque differential magnetometry using the qPlus mode of a quartz tuning fork. The tuning fork is driven by an ac voltage, and its deflection is measured by the resultant ac current. We observe a sharp resonance of the quartz tuning fork at low temperatures down to 1.6 K. We calibrate our torque differential magnetometry by measuring the angular dependence of the hysteresis loop in single-crystal \(\mathrm{Fe}_{0.25}\mathrm{TaS}_{2}\). Furthermore, we demonstrate the high sensitivity of the torque differential magnetometry by measuring the quantum oscillations of a bismuth single crystal. The extracted Fermi-surface cross sections are consistent with those of bismuth crystals.

publications.title: Bulk Rotational Symmetry Breaking in Kondo Insulator \(\mathbf{SmB_6}\)
publications.authors: Z. Xiang, B. Lawson, T. Asaba, C. Tinsman, Lu Chen, C. Shang, X. H. Chen, Lu Li
publications.abstract: The Kondo insulator samarium hexaboride (\(\mathrm{SmB}_{6}\)) has been intensely studied in recent years as a potential candidate of a strongly correlated topological insulator. One of the most exciting phenomena observed in \(\mathrm{SmB}_{6}\) is the clear quantum oscillations appearing in magnetic torque at a low temperature despite the insulating behavior in resistance. These quantum oscillations show multiple frequencies and varied effective masses. The origin of quantum oscillation is, however, still under debate with evidence of both two-dimensional Fermi surfaces and three-dimensional Fermi surfaces. Here, we carry out angle-resolved torque magnetometry measurements in a magnetic field up to 45 T and a temperature range down to 40 mK. With the magnetic field rotated in the (010) plane, the quantum oscillation frequency of the strongest oscillation branch shows a fourfold rotational symmetry. However, in the angular dependence of the amplitude of the same branch, this fourfold symmetry is broken and, instead, a twofold symmetry shows up, which is consistent with the prediction of a two-dimensional Lifshitz-Kosevich model. No deviation of Lifshitz-Kosevich behavior is observed down to 40 mK. Our results suggest the existence of multiple light-mass surface states in \(\mathrm{SmB}_{6}\), with their mobility significantly depending on the surface disorder level.

publications.title: Rotational Symmetry Breaking in a Trigonal Superconductor Nb-doped \(\mathbf{Bi_2Se_3}\)
publications.authors: Tomoya Asaba, B. J. Lawson, Colin Tinsman, Lu Chen, Paul Corbae, Gang Li, Y. Qiu, Y. S. Hor, Liang Fu, Lu Li
publications.abstract: The search for unconventional superconductivity has been focused on materials with strong spin-orbit coupling and unique crystal lattices. Doped bismuth selenide (\(\mathrm{Bi}_{2}\mathrm{Se}_{3}\)) is a strong candidate, given the topological insulator nature of the parent compound and its triangular lattice. The coupling between the physical properties in the superconducting state and its underlying crystal symmetry is a crucial test for unconventional superconductivity. In this paper, we report direct evidence that the superconducting magnetic response couples strongly to the underlying trigonal crystal symmetry in the recently discovered superconductor with trigonal crystal structure, niobium (Nb)-doped \(\mathrm{Bi}_{2}\mathrm{Se}_{3}\). As a result, the in-plane magnetic torque signal vanishes every 60°. More importantly, the superconducting hysteresis loop amplitude is enhanced along one preferred direction, spontaneously breaking the rotational symmetry. This observation indicates the presence of nematic order in the superconducting ground state of Nb-doped \(\mathrm{Bi}_{2}\mathrm{Se}_{3}\).

publications.title: Doping-Dependent Photon Scattering Resonance in the Model High-Temperature Superconductor \(\mathbf{HgBa_2CuO_{4+\delta}}\) Revealed by Raman Scattering and Optical Ellipsometry
publications.authors: Yuan Li, M. Le Tacon, Y. Matiks, A. V. Boris, T. Loew, C. T. Lin, Lu Chen, M. K. Chan, C. Dorow, L. Ji, N. Barišić, X. Zhao, M. Greven, B. Keimer
publications.abstract: We study the model high-temperature superconductor \(\mathrm{HgBa}_{2}\mathrm{CuO}_{4+\delta}\) with electronic Raman scattering and optical ellipsometry over a wide doping range. The dependence of the resonant Raman cross section on the incident photon energy changes drastically as a function of doping, in a manner that corresponds to a rearrangement of the interband optical transitions seen with ellipsometry. This doping-dependent Raman resonance allows us to reconcile the apparent discrepancy between Raman and x-ray detection of magnetic fluctuations in superconducting cuprates. Intriguingly, the strongest variation occurs across the doping level where the antinodal superconducting gap appears to reach its maximum.

items.imageAlt: First MagLab trip with two people standing above a measurement setup
items.title: Trip to MagLab
items.summary: Lu and her graduate student Lingyu ran the group's first experiment at [National High Magnetic Field Laboratory](https://nationalmaglab.org) in Tallahassee, FL. This experiment is part of the collaboration work with [Professor Analytis's Group](https://physics.berkeley.edu/research-faculty/research-groups/analytis-lab) in UC Berkeley. Lu and Lingyu ran measurements on unconventional superconductors up to 41 Tesla and down to He-3 temperature. The data may further advance researchers' understanding of the Fermi Surface Structure of these materials.

items.imageAlt: New concrete floor work in MRL B09 for the measurement lab
items.title: Concrete work finished in MRL B09
items.summary: Our renovation team at MRL has just completed the floor work on both the south and north sides of B09. Metal rebars have been removed and replaced with new concrete. The space is now ready to welcome our Teslatron PT and ProteoxMX dilution refrigerator from Quantum Design Oxford. Many thanks to MRL and PHYSICS for making this happen.

items.imageAlt: MRL B09 concrete preparation work underway
items.title: Concrete work started in MRL B09
items.summary: Good news! Our renovation team at MRL has started the key preparation steps for our measurement lab at MRL B09, cutting concrete and removing metal rebars in the ground. Then the space will be ready to welcome our Teslatron PT and ProteoxMX dilution refrigerator from Quantum Design Oxford. TEST

items.title: Gavin joins the group as an undergraduate student

items.title: Jack joins the group as a PhD student
items.summary: Jack has joined the lab and will develop a comprehensive data acquisition program for the 14 T Teslatron PT system.

items.title: Vedang joins the group as an undergraduate student
items.summary: Vedang has joined the lab and will build a low-noise nanovolt DC amplifier for the thermal transport system.

items.title: Sample prep lab is ready
items.summary: Renovation of the sample preparation lab at MRL 234 is complete. The updated space supports sample preparation, and sample mounting equipment is being brought online.

items.title: Lingyu joins the group as a PhD student
items.summary: Lingyu has joined the lab and will work on designing and assembling the cold finger in the dilution refrigerator and quantum oscillation measurements in unconventional superconductors.

items.title: Wenjie joins the group as an undergraduate student
items.summary: Wenjie (Michael) has joined the lab and will work on designing and assembling a thermal transport puck in the PPMS system.
